title: Supplier On-Time In-Full (OTIF) Delivery Performance
dataSources: SAP MM chain — Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686) JDBC extracts into `index=business`: goods-receipt confirmations `sourcetype="dbx:sap_ekbe"` keyed from table **EKBE** (`MOVEMENT_TYPE=101` GR lines; fields such as `PO_NUMBER`, `ITEM_NUMBER`, `POSTING_DATE`, `QUANTITY`, `VENDOR`, `MATL_DOC`), joined to scheduling promises `sourcetype="dbx:sap_eket"` sourced from **EKET** (`PROMISED_DELIVERY_DATE`, `COMMITTED_QTY`). Alternate ERP lanes: Oracle **RCV_TRANSACTIONS** / **PO_LINE_LOCATIONS_ALL** via `dbx:oracle:rcv` + `dbx:oracle:po`; Dynamics **INVENTTRANS** receipt joins over `dbx:d365:purchtable`. SaaS overlay — Coupa REST `GET /api/purchase_orders` with `need_by_date`, `requested_delivery_date`, plus `GET /api/order_header_receipts` ingested as `coupa:purchase_order` and `coupa:receipt` from a Heavy Forwarder modular input using OAuth2 client_credentials (Splunkbase lists DB Connect 2686; Coupa/Ariba polling typically ships as custom modular inputs). SAP Ariba Network buyer payloads (`ariba:order`) normalize separately before blending vendor IDs with ERP keys.
app: Splunk DB Connect ([Splunkbase 2686](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2686)), package `splunk_app_db_connect`, on Heavy Forwarder or dedicated DB Connect worker; Splunk Add-on for SAP HANA ([Splunkbase 4045](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4045)) optional when JDBC targets native HANA SQL views for **EKBE**/**EKET**. Pair with organization-built modular inputs or HEC receivers for Coupa (`coupa:purchase_order`, `coupa:receipt`) and Ariba (`ariba:order`) client_credentials flows — no single Splunk-certified TA covers every SaaS procurement tenant as of 2026.
spl:
index=business sourcetype="dbx:sap_ekbe" MOVEMENT_TYPE=101 earliest=-180d
| eval gr_date=strptime(POSTING_DATE,"%Y-%m-%d")
| join type=left PO_NUMBER, ITEM_NUMBER [
    search index=business sourcetype="dbx:sap_eket"
    | eval promised=strptime(PROMISED_DELIVERY_DATE,"%Y-%m-%d")
    | rename COMMITTED_QTY as qty_ordered
    | fields PO_NUMBER, ITEM_NUMBER, promised, qty_ordered ]
| stats sum(QUANTITY) as qty_received, max(gr_date) as last_receipt_date, max(promised) as promised_date, max(qty_ordered) as qty_ordered by PO_NUMBER, ITEM_NUMBER, VENDOR
| eval days_late=round((last_receipt_date - promised_date)/86400, 0)
| eval on_time=if(days_late <= 0, 1, 0)
| eval in_full=if(qty_received >= qty_ordered, 1, 0)
| eval otif=if(on_time=1 AND in_full=1, 1, 0)
| lookup supplier_classification.csv VENDOR OUTPUT supplier_tier, otif_target
| stats count as deliveries, sum(on_time) as on_time_count, sum(in_full) as in_full_count, sum(otif) as otif_count, avg(days_late) as avg_days_late, values(supplier_tier) as supplier_tier, values(otif_target) as otif_target by VENDOR
| eval otif_pct=round(100*otif_count/deliveries, 1)
| eval on_time_pct=round(100*on_time_count/deliveries, 1)
| eval in_full_pct=round(100*in_full_count/deliveries, 1)
| eval gap_to_target=round(otif_pct - otif_target, 1)
| eval status=case(
    deliveries < 5, "Insufficient data",
    gap_to_target >= 0, "Meeting target",
    gap_to_target >= -5, "Approaching",
    1=1, "Below target — escalate")
| sort - deliveries
| table VENDOR, supplier_tier, deliveries, otif_pct, on_time_pct, in_full_pct, otif_target, gap_to_target, avg_days_late, status
description: Computes procurement OTIF from Splunk-ingested GR rows versus promised schedule quantities — SAP pattern joins **EKBE** movement-type 101 receipts on `(PO_NUMBER,ITEM_NUMBER)` to **EKET** promised clocks and committed quantities, flags each vendor-line receipt as on-time (`last_receipt_date` versus `promised_date` within tolerance baked into dates), in-full (`qty_received` versus `qty_ordered`), then OTIF where both hold; rolls up vendor grain after **supplier_classification.csv** pulls Kraljic-style tier targets so dashboards expose gap-to-target for scorecards distinct from downstream order-to-cash dwell or warehouse-on-hand snapshots.
value: Chief Procurement Officers and category managers steer QBR scorecards, contractual remedies, and dual-sourcing moves using one KPI aligned with APICS-style benchmarks — top-decile OTIF sits above ninety-five percent while many manufacturing programs plateau between seventy and eighty-five percent; recovering five OTIF percentage points against a hundred-million-dollar spend band typically frees about one to two million dollars in avoided expedites, premium freight, line-down penalties, and lost-margin stockouts rather than debating anecdotes at negotiation tables.
implementation: (1) Land DB Connect JDBC pulls for SAP **EKBE**/**EKET** (or Oracle/Dynamics equivalents) plus Coupa/Ariba modular-input feeds into `index=business`; (2) publish `supplier_classification.csv` mapping vendor IDs to tier labels and numeric OTIF targets (ninety-eight leverage, ninety bottleneck, eighty-five non-critical); (3) save the bundled SPL as supplier-scorecard panels with weekly cadence for sourcing councils and threshold alerts where `gap_to_target` breaches procurement policy.
visualization: Supplier scorecard table sorted by OTIF versus tier targets; horizontal bar chart of OTIF percentage by supplier with a constant reference line at enterprise OTIF minimum; heatmap faceting supplier × calendar month on OTIF percentage for seasonal regressions; single-value headline for fleet OTIF plus week-over-week delta; trend line or area chart for rolling thirty-day OTIF split by supplier tier overlays.

references.title: Coupa Developer Portal — Purchase Order APIs
ASCM — Supply chain metrics education hub

knownFalsePositives: Buyer-initiated PO quantity bumps leave **EKET** promised dates stale while **EKBE** receipts track new totals — OTIF reads falsely late until confirmations replay. Dock clerks batch **MIGO** postings one to three days after physical receipt so GR timestamps drift though trailers arrived on schedule. Supplier acknowledgement categories (**AB** versus **LA**) stamp different baseline dates — dashboards anchored only on order acknowledgement mis-score OTIF versus logistics-aware notices. Mixed pallets through ocean consolidation hubs tie receipts to hub arrival windows rather than promised PO-line milestones once freight-forward data absent. Materials parked in SAP quality inspection buckets appear unreleased-to-stock though pallets landed — procurement argues OTIF satisfied while planners still starved downstream when QC gates linger. Multiple GR documents against one PO line demand summed quantities — Splunk tallies fall short when reversal movements (**MOVEMENT_TYPE** 102) net incompletely across polls. Drop-ship lanes bypass hub GR entirely — receipts never arrive where dashboards expect warehouse postings; mirror Coupa receipt confirmations instead of ERP GR rows only. Vendor-managed inventory programs replace discrete PO cycles — OTIF denominators evaporate though service stays acceptable. Buyer calendars declare holidays supplier calendars ignore — dock closures skew perceived lateness near weekends. Midnight timezone normalization compares UTC-cut promises against plant-local receipts creating ± one-day jitter at fiscal boundaries. Split-line shipments score blended OTIF when line two slips though line one carries the headline vendor KPI. Quarter-end pushes concentrating receipts inflate OTIF optics versus steady rhythm buyers expected. SaaS lifecycle labels (**Coupa** Closed versus Partially Received versus **Ariba** Received) mismatch ERP GR counts until normalization lookups reconcile shipment states. ASN (**EDI 856**) arrival timestamps diverge from dock sign-offs — variance signals paperwork friction rather than carrier punctuality alone. FX snapshots comparing promised-value-only checks versus GR-period valuation disturb full-dollar fulfillment logic though quantities reconcile. Macro disruptions such as Suez congestion or fleet-wide canal delays crater cohort OTIF regardless of supplier tactics — segregate force-majeure buckets before punitive reviews.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud search heads aligned with splunkVersions above; Heavy Forwarder or isolated DB Connect worker permitted outbound JDBC toward SAP HANA (`jdbc:sap://host:3NN15`), Oracle RAC listeners (`1521/tcp`), SQL Server replicas hosting curated procurement extracts — firewall approvals referenced on change tickets.
• Splunk DB Connect identities restricted to SELECT-only roles hitting staging views projecting **EKBE**/**EKET** columns (`Splunkbase 2686`) plus optional Splunk Add-on for SAP HANA (`Splunkbase 4045`) when Basis mandates native **ngdbc.jar** connectivity.
• OAuth2 secrets stored outside searchable configs for Coupa modular inputs hitting `/api/purchase_orders` and `/api/order_header_receipts` token endpoints — rotate quarterly.
• Lookup governance owning `supplier_classification.csv` keyed on canonical vendor identifiers (`VENDOR`, `supplier_number`) including tier labels derived from Kraljic segmentation workshops so OTIF targets align with contracting clauses.
• Splunk roles granting `srchIndexesAllowed` over `business` without exposing unrelated payroll extracts sitting alongside ERP clones.

Step 1 — Configure ERP JDBC inputs and SaaS receivers
Stand up DB Connect rising-column polls (`cron_schedule = 0 */4 * * *` typical) emitting sourcetypes `dbx:sap_ekbe` and `dbx:sap_eket` sourced from SAP extraction SQL selecting GR rows (`MOVEMENT_TYPE IN ('101','102')`) — exclude noisy reversal duplicates downstream via SPL nets unless Finance insists gross postings—paired extracts sharing watermark columns (`LASTCHANGEDATETIME`) via staging tables replicated near-real-time from **EKKO**/**EKPO** joins maintained by SAP MM analysts.

Oracle alternative wires `[oracle://corp/rs_rcv_po]` feeds referencing **RCV_TRANSACTIONS** (`TRANSACTION_TYPE='RECEIVE'`) merged with **PO_LINE_LOCATIONS_ALL** promised clocks.

Dynamics alternative publishes **INVENTTRANS** physical-date receipts keyed back to **PURCHLINE** quantities.

Modular Coupa/Ariba collectors authenticate client_credentials against issuer URLs documented in tenant setup guides; emit JSON preserving supplier identifiers reconcilable with ERP vendor masters (`lifnr`-normalized).

Props Layer stamps `_time` from operational GR posting timestamps (`POSTING_DATE`) — not indexer arrival — so OTIF aligns with SAP transaction **MB51** material-document listings purchasers audit manually.

Step 2 — Build the OTIF measurement search
Reuse the SPL packaged in this UC verbatim before customizing tolerance macros.

The outer search binds SAP GR rows (`MOVEMENT_TYPE=101`) across one hundred eighty rolling days — reversal hygiene belongs in preprocessing — translating posting dates through `strptime` into `gr_date`.

The left join pulls scheduling commitments keyed on `(PO_NUMBER,ITEM_NUMBER)` from **EKET**, renaming committed quantities `qty_ordered` so downstream comparisons remain procurement-legible.

Aggregating through `stats` collapses duplicate GR postings into summed receipts (`qty_received`), retains latest receipt timestamp (`last_receipt_date`), promised epoch (`promised_date`), and baseline ordered quantity — grain stays PO-line × vendor.

Derived metrics subtract epochs into whole-day latency (`days_late`), derive binary punctuality (`on_time`), completeness (`in_full`), then OTIF conjunction.

The vendor lookup attaches tier-dependent OTIF objectives — ninety-eight strategic, ninety-five leverage per exemplar governance tables — enabling `gap_to_target` storytelling procurement committees already cite.

Vendor-level summarization respects minimum-sample guards (`deliveries < 5`) preventing punitive escalations against sporadic vendors.

Step 3 — Validate against ERP truth
Verify against the SAP ME23N dashboard UI that scheduling-line quantities and promised dates tie back to Splunk row-level sums before trusting punitive OTIF deltas.
Inside SAP GUI compare Splunk-highlighted outliers via transaction ME23N scheduling lines versus MB51 lists tied to identical material-document numbers surfaced in Splunk (`MATL_DOC`). Confirm summed receipt quantities reconcile when sequential GR movements reflect partial shipments.

Verify against the Oracle Purchasing dashboard UI — JDBC totals must equal inquiry screens filtered by receipt numbers mirrored from RCV_SHIPMENT_HEADERS identifiers.

Verify against the Coupa procurement dashboard UI — reconcile promised versus receipt timestamps exported via Splunk drill links keyed on PO identifiers before supplier scorecards finalize.

Back-test OTIF percentages across rolling quarters versus supplier scorecards Finance already publishes — deviations imply watermark lag (`_indextime - _time` skew) rather than arithmetic drift.

Step 4 — Operationalize dashboards and accountability
Dashboard Studio composition recommendation includes supplier-level splunk.table tiles exposing OTIF percentage, punctuality-only versus fulfillment-only splits, tier alignment, gap-to-target coloring consistent with sourcing council palettes.

Pair horizontal bar charts annotating ninety-five percent benchmark overlays sourced from ASCM literature referenced externally.

Heatmaps illuminate seasonal softness — procurement planners anticipate inbound volatility holidays ignoring warehouse outbound KPI companions tracked elsewhere.

Rolling thirty-day trending segmented by tier overlays reinforces Kraljic guidance—strategic suppliers receive stricter escalation paths when below ninety-eight percent.

Route `status` Below target rows to category owners through ServiceNow queues; hold executive review only when tier-one suppliers stay negative `gap_to_target` consecutive weeks after verified data quality checks complete.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting hard procurement-edge noise
• No GR events indexed for sourcetype dbx:sap_ekbe across entire lookbacks usually indicates JDBC watermark stalls or Basis replay pauses — inspect DB Connect task logs before accusing suppliers.
• Coupa API timeout errors during nightly polls strand modular-input checkpoints until operators rerun backfills — widen intervals or shrink batch windows.
• JDBC pools exhausting worker threads manifest duplicate-null pulls — inspect DB Connect operational dashboards before accusing suppliers.
• Missing **MOVEMENT_TYPE** fields imply extracts reused invoice-related postings accidentally — tighten DB SQL predicates upstream.
• Coupa OAuth returning HTTP 429 signals polling cadence aggression — backoff intervals (`interval = 900`) and watermark spacing relieve throttles.
• `lookup` misses on `supplier_classification.csv` yield null `otif_target` gaps — reconcile vendor ID formatting between ERP leading zeros and SaaS alphanumeric keys through transform rules.
• Reversal floods after month-end corrections double-count negatives when 102 movements fail to ingest — Splunk totals understate receipts; cross-check reversal net blocks with SAP analysts.
• Timezone mismatches near international plants show chronic one-day misses — harmonize extraction SQL to warehouse local zone when **EKET** stores calendar dates without offsets.
• VMI programs still ingesting phantom PO lines produce zero denominators — exclude `vmi_flag` rows through subsearch macros once master data exposes consignment indicators.
• Cross-system normalization failing between Ariba lifecycle states and ERP GR postings inflates apparent partials — enforce mapping table between `STATUS` enumerations before OTIF ratios feed leadership.
• Force-majeure events dominating entire cohorts should reference external disruption tags so supplier development programs distinguish systemic shock from chronic underperformance.
grandmaExplanation: We check whether suppliers keep their delivery promises — both on the day they said and with the full amount we ordered — and show which ones miss often enough to hurt production. That way we negotiate fair contracts and help the right vendors before shortages cost big money.
